evals.prompt: I want to run /rune:devise for a new feature: 'Add OAuth2 authentication with Google and GitHub providers'. Walk me through what happens in Phase -1 (Team Bootstrap) and Phase 0 (Gather Input). Explain: (1) What the teamTransition protocol does, (2) How ATE-1 enforcement is enabled, (3) What happens if brainstorm-context is NOT provided vs if it IS provided, (4) How design signal detection works for Figma URLs. Don't actually execute anything — just explain the decision tree.
evals.expected_output: A detailed walkthrough of: (1) Team Bootstrap — the 6-step teamTransition protocol (validate → TeamDelete with retry → filesystem fallback → TeamCreate with 'Already leading' recovery → post-create verification → state file write with config_dir, owner_pid, session_id). (2) ATE-1 enforcement — enabled by team creation BEFORE any Agent() calls, all subsequent Agent() calls must include team_name. (3) Phase 0 paths — default: delegate to brainstorm protocol with devise-specific overrides (advisors join existing team, workspace co-located, skips handoff); --brainstorm-context PATH: read existing workspace, skip brainstorm, inject context; --quick: skip brainstorm, ask for feature description via AskUserQuestion. (4) Design signal detection — FIGMA_URL_PATTERN scan, set designAware flag, conditionally spawn design-pipeline-agent with figma_list_components MCP.
expectations: Describes the 6-step teamTransition protocol in Phase -1
Mentions TeamCreate with 'Already leading' recovery pattern
Explains state file includes config_dir, owner_pid, session_id for session isolation
Describes ATE-1 enforcement: team created before any Agent() calls, all Agents need team_name
Explains the three Phase 0 paths: default (brainstorm), --brainstorm-context, --quick
Notes that brainstorm advisors join the devise team (not a separate team)
Describes FIGMA_URL_PATTERN scanning for design signal detection
Mentions design-pipeline-agent spawns conditionally when Figma URLs detected

evals.prompt: Analyze the research phase decision tree for devise. I have a feature that uses React 19 and Next.js 15. Explain: (1) What local research agents always run? (2) How does Phase 1B decide whether to run external research? (3) What talisman config controls external research bypass? (4) How does Context7 MCP integrate with lore-scholar? (5) What happens in Phase 1C.5 research verification? (6) What triggers the research consolidation checkpoint in Phase 1.5?
evals.expected_output: Detailed Phase 1 research analysis: (1) Local research always runs: repo-surveyor (codebase patterns), echo-reader (Rune Echoes), git-miner (git history). (2) Phase 1B decision: risk score + local sufficiency scoring, reads talisman plan.external_research for bypass modes (always/auto/never), URL sanitization for SSRF defense. (3) talisman.yml plan.external_research: 'always' forces external, 'never' skips, 'auto' uses decision logic. (4) Context7 MCP: lore-scholar uses resolve-library-id then query-docs for framework-specific docs. (5) Phase 1C.5: research-verifier scores findings on 5 dimensions (relevance, accuracy, freshness, cross-validation, security), maps to verdicts (TRUSTED/CAUTION/UNTRUSTED/FLAGGED). (6) Phase 1.5 checkpoint: AskUserQuestion after all research tasks converge, validates consolidation before proceeding.
expectations: Lists the 3 local research agents: repo-surveyor, echo-reader, git-miner
Explains Phase 1B decision uses risk + local sufficiency scoring
Mentions talisman plan.external_research bypass modes: always/auto/never
Notes SSRF-defensive URL sanitization in Phase 1B
Describes Context7 MCP integration: resolve-library-id then query-docs
Lists the 5 research verification dimensions
Maps the 4 verdicts: TRUSTED/CAUTION/UNTRUSTED/FLAGGED
Describes Phase 1.5 AskUserQuestion checkpoint for consolidation validation

evals.prompt: I'm running /rune:devise --quick for a bug fix. Explain what phases are skipped and why. Then explain what happens if I also pass --no-arena but don't pass --quick. Also explain the --exhaustive flag for forge mode and when it auto-triggers.
evals.expected_output: Flag impact analysis: --quick skips: Phase 0 brainstorm (asks for description directly), Phase 1.8 Solution Arena, Phase 2.3 Predictive Goldmask, Phase 2.5 Shatter Assessment, Phase 3 Forge. These are skipped because --quick is designed for straightforward tasks that don't need competitive evaluation or deep enrichment. --no-arena alone skips only Phase 1.8 Solution Arena — useful when you want forge enrichment but know there's only one viable approach. --exhaustive forge mode: threshold 0.15 (vs 0.30 default), max 5 agents/section (vs 3), max 12 total (vs 8), includes research-budget agents, two-tier aggregation, cost warning before summoning. Auto-triggers when user message contains ULTRATHINK, DEEP, or ARCHITECT keywords.
expectations: Lists phases skipped by --quick: brainstorm, arena, predictive goldmask, shatter, forge
Explains --quick is for straightforward tasks without competitive evaluation
Notes --no-arena skips only Phase 1.8 Solution Arena
Describes --exhaustive thresholds: 0.15 vs 0.30, 5 agents/section vs 3, 12 total vs 8
Mentions --exhaustive includes research-budget agents and two-tier aggregation
Lists auto-trigger keywords: ULTRATHINK, DEEP, ARCHITECT
Notes cost warning is shown before summoning in exhaustive mode
Explains the difference between --quick (multi-phase skip) vs granular flags (--no-arena, --no-forge)

evals.prompt: Explain the Solution Arena phase (1.8) in detail. When does it run vs skip? What are the 4 sub-steps (1.8A through 1.8D)? How does the recovery protocol work if all solutions are killed? What is the complexity gate?
evals.expected_output: Solution Arena analysis: Skip conditions: --quick, --no-arena, bug fixes, high-confidence refactors (confidence >= 0.9), sparse research (<2 viable approaches). 4 sub-steps: 1.8A COMPLEXITY GATE + GENERATE — check skip conditions, then create 2-5 competing solutions from research; 1.8B CHALLENGE — adversarial agents (Devil's Advocate, Innovation Scout) attack solutions; 1.8C CONSOLIDATE & SCORE — build weighted evaluation matrix on 6 dimensions; 1.8D SELECT — present decision matrix for user approach selection. Recovery protocol: if all solutions killed, offer (a) proceed with least-flawed, (b) return to research (max 1 retry), or (c) abandon feature. Complexity gate: evaluated at start of 1.8A to determine if Arena should run based on skip conditions. Output: arena-selection.md with winning solution and rationale.
expectations: Lists skip conditions: --quick, --no-arena, bug fixes, high-confidence refactors, sparse research
Notes high-confidence threshold is >= 0.9
Notes sparse research threshold is <2 viable approaches
Describes 1.8A: Complexity gate check + generate competing solutions from research
Describes 1.8B CHALLENGE: adversarial agents (Devil's Advocate, Innovation Scout) attack solutions
Describes 1.8C CONSOLIDATE & SCORE: build weighted evaluation matrix on 6 dimensions
Describes 1.8D SELECT: present decision matrix for user approach selection
Explains recovery protocol: proceed with least-flawed, return to research (max 1 retry), or abandon
Mentions complexity gate is evaluated at start of 1.8A
Notes output is arena-selection.md

evals.prompt: Analyze the Phase 2.5 Shatter Assessment algorithm. What signals are scored and their weights? When is shatter offered vs skipped? What is the difference between shard generation and hierarchical decomposition? How does coherence checking work?
evals.expected_output: Shatter Assessment breakdown: Signal weights: Task count (40%, threshold >=8), Phase count (30%, threshold >=3), Cross-cutting concerns (20%, threshold >=2 shared deps), Estimated effort (10%, threshold >=2 L-size phases). Offer shatter when score >= 0.65, skip below. Shard generation: horizontal split into parallel shards (shard-N files), each shard is independent plan. Hierarchical decomposition: parent/child structure with dependency DAG, requires/provides contracts, feature branch per child. Coherence checking: validates that shards/children together cover full scope, no orphan requirements, no circular dependencies. Output: either original plan, shard files, or child plan references in parent.
expectations: Lists 4 signals with weights: Task count 40%, Phase count 30%, Cross-cutting concerns 20%, Estimated effort 10%
States the offer threshold: score >= 0.65
Describes shard generation: horizontal split into parallel shards (shard-N files)
Describes hierarchical decomposition: parent/child with dependency DAG, requires/provides contracts
Notes hierarchical uses feature branch per child
Explains coherence checking: validates full scope coverage, no orphans, no circular deps
Mentions the score calculation combines all 4 signals

evals.prompt: Walk me through Phase 6 Cleanup for devise. What are the 5 components of the standard cleanup pattern? What is the fallback array and why does it need to be complete? How does SEC-4 validation work? What is QUAL-012?
evals.expected_output: Phase 6 Cleanup breakdown: 5 components: (1) Dynamic member discovery — read team config for all teammates; (2) shutdown_request to all members via SendMessage; (3) Grace period — let teammates deregister (20s); (4) TeamDelete with retry-with-backoff (4 attempts: 0s, 3s, 6s, 10s); (5) Filesystem fallback — only if TeamDelete never succeeded. Fallback array: hardcoded list of ALL possible teammates for the workflow, covers conditional phases, safe to send shutdown_request to absent members. SEC-4 validation: filter member names with /^[a-zA-Z0-9_-]+$/ before use in SendMessage (prevents injection). QUAL-012: gate filesystem fallback behind !cleanupTeamDeleteSucceeded — avoid unnecessary rm -rf when TeamDelete already succeeded. Also includes workflow lock release.
expectations: Lists 5 cleanup components in order: discovery, shutdown_request, grace period, TeamDelete retry, filesystem fallback
Notes grace period is 20 seconds
Lists TeamDelete retry delays: 0s, 3s, 6s, 10s (4 attempts)
Explains fallback array covers all conditional phases
Describes SEC-4 validation: /^[a-zA-Z0-9_-]+$/ regex for member names
Explains QUAL-012: gate filesystem fallback behind !cleanupTeamDeleteSucceeded
Notes workflow lock is released as part of cleanup
Mentions CHOME pattern for multi-account support

evals.prompt: I have a brainstorm workspace at tmp/brainstorm-20260307/ with workspace-meta.json showing quality_score: 0.85. I want to run /rune:devise --brainstorm-context tmp/brainstorm-20260307/. What happens differently compared to running devise without this flag? How does the quality score affect the workflow? What context gets injected into Phase 1 research agents?
evals.expected_output: Brainstorm-context flow: Skips Phase 0 brainstorm entirely (no advisor summoning). Reads workspace-meta.json for quality_score (0.85 >= 0.70 = high-confidence). Injects into Phase 1 research agents: advisor research outputs, brainstorm decisions, constraint classification, success criteria. Quality score 0.85 means high-confidence mode — research agents proceed with stronger priors, less exploration needed. If score was < 0.70, would flag as 'exploratory' and suggest additional validation. The brainstorm-decisions.md sections (Non-Goals, Constraint Classification, Success Criteria, Scope Boundary) become inputs to synthesis. Design-aware flags and Figma URLs from brainstorm are also passed through.
expectations: Notes Phase 0 brainstorm is skipped entirely
Explains quality_score 0.85 >= 0.70 triggers high-confidence mode
Describes what gets injected: advisor research, decisions, constraints, success criteria
Notes < 0.70 would flag as 'exploratory'
Mentions brainstorm-decisions.md sections become synthesis inputs
Notes design-aware flags and Figma URLs are passed through

evals.prompt: Explain the Forge phase (Phase 3) in detail. What is Forge Gaze? How does topic-aware agent matching work? What is the truthbinding pattern with ANCHOR/RE-ANCHOR blocks? How does the fallback work when no agent scores above threshold?
evals.expected_output: Forge phase analysis: Forge Gaze is topic-aware agent matching — parses plan sections, matches topics to specialized Ashes. Topic matching: registry maps keywords/topics to agent capabilities, threshold 0.30 for default mode, max 3 agents per section. Truthbinding: ANCHOR block at start of prompt (original plan content), RE-ANCHOR blocks during iteration (reference back to anchor), plan content sanitized (strip HTML comments, code fences, headings, entities, zero-width chars) before injection to prevent prompt injection. Fallback: if no agent scores above threshold, use inline generic Task prompt for standard enrichment. Elicitation sages: up to MAX_FORGE_SAGES=6 per eligible section with keyword pre-filter. Summon throttle: max 5 concurrent, max 8 total agents.
expectations: Describes Forge Gaze: topic-aware agent matching for plan sections
Notes threshold 0.30 for default mode
Notes max 3 agents per section in default mode
Describes ANCHOR block for original plan content
Describes RE-ANCHOR blocks for iteration reference
Lists sanitization steps: strip HTML comments, code fences, headings, entities, zero-width chars
Explains fallback: generic Task prompt when no agent above threshold
Notes MAX_FORGE_SAGES=6 with keyword pre-filter
Notes summon throttle: 5 concurrent, 8 total

evals.prompt: What error handling exists in the devise workflow? Explain what happens when: (1) A research agent times out (>5 min), (2) Solution Arena has sparse research (<2 approaches), (3) Forge agent times out, (4) TeamCreate fails with 'Already leading', (5) Plan review returns BLOCK verdict.
evals.expected_output: Error handling matrix: (1) Research timeout: proceed with partial research, log gap, continue to synthesis with available data. (2) Sparse research (<2 approaches): skip Arena entirely, proceed directly to Phase 2 synthesize with available research. (3) Forge timeout (>5 min): proceed with partial enrichment, use what completed, don't block pipeline. (4) TeamCreate 'Already leading': catch-and-recover via teamTransition protocol — check for existing team, validate ownership, either join existing or force cleanup. (5) Plan review BLOCK: address blocking issues before presenting plan, max 2 refinement passes for HIGH severity issues. All errors are non-blocking where possible — devise prefers partial output over abort.
expectations: Research timeout: proceed with partial research
Sparse research: skip Arena, proceed to synthesize
Forge timeout: proceed with partial enrichment
TeamCreate 'Already leading': catch-and-recover via teamTransition
Plan review BLOCK: address issues before presenting, max 2 refinement passes
Notes devise prefers partial output over abort
Mentions research verification timeout proceeds with unverified + warning

evals.prompt: Explain the session isolation requirements for devise state files. What fields must be included? How do hook scripts verify ownership? What happens when a state file belongs to another live session vs a dead session?
evals.expected_output: Session isolation analysis: State files MUST include: config_dir (CLAUDE_CONFIG_DIR path for installation isolation), owner_pid (Claude Code process PID via $PPID for session isolation), session_id (CLAUDE_SESSION_ID for diagnostics). Hook scripts must: (1) Check config_dir matches current session before acting, (2) Check owner_pid matches $PPID with kill -0 liveness check, (3) Skip silently if state belongs to another live session, (4) Clean up if state belongs to a dead session (orphan recovery). This prevents cross-session interference when multiple Claude Code sessions work on the same repo.
expectations: Lists 3 required fields: config_dir, owner_pid, session_id
Explains config_dir is for installation isolation
Explains owner_pid is for session isolation via $PPID
Mentions kill -0 liveness check for PID verification
Describes skip silently behavior for another live session
Describes cleanup behavior for dead session (orphan recovery)
Notes this prevents cross-session interference

evals.prompt: How does the Predictive Goldmask phase (2.3) work? What are the 3 depth modes and the v3.x default? When is it skipped?
evals.expected_output: Predictive Goldmask analysis: Depth modes: basic (2 agents), enhanced (6 agents), full (8 agents). v3.x bakes the depth at 'basic' (see references/v3-defaults.md — the goldmask.devise.depth knob). Skip conditions: --quick mode, non-git repo. Goldmask + the devise predictive layer are always enabled in v3.x. Purpose: runs predictive risk analysis on files likely affected by the plan before implementation. Outputs risk scores and wisdom advisories that get injected into the plan. Non-blocking on failure — proceeds with partial data or skips injection.
expectations: Lists 3 depth modes: basic (2 agents), enhanced (6 agents), full (8 agents)
Notes 'basic' is the v3.x baked default and points to references/v3-defaults.md
Lists skip conditions: --quick, non-git repo
Notes goldmask + devise predictive layer are always on in v3.x
Notes non-blocking behavior on failure
Mentions risk scores and wisdom advisories are outputs

evals.prompt: How does Phase 0.3 UX Pattern Analysis work? When does it run? What conditions must be met? What output does it produce for Phase 2 synthesis?
evals.expected_output: UX Pattern Analysis (Phase 0.3): Conditions: frontend files detected (src/components/**/*.{tsx,jsx}, components/**/*.{tsx,jsx}, or app/**/*.{tsx,jsx} > 10 files). The UX subsystem is retained for inspector heuristics in v3.x with no user knobs (see references/v3-defaults.md). Spawns ux-pattern-analyzer agent to inventory existing UX patterns (7 categories: loading, error handling, form validation, navigation, empty states, confirmation/undo, feedback). Output: brainstormContext.ux_maturity object with analyzed: true, process type, cognitive_walkthrough flag. Used by Phase 2 synthesis to include 'UX Considerations' section in plan. Skipped when no frontend files are detected.
expectations: Identifies the trigger condition: frontend files detected (>10 components)
Lists frontend file patterns: src/components, components, app directories
Names the agent: ux-pattern-analyzer
Lists 7 UX pattern categories
Describes the output: brainstormContext.ux_maturity object
Notes this feeds into Phase 2 synthesis for UX Considerations section

evals.prompt: Explain how plan output filenames are generated. What is the naming convention? Give examples for different feature types. What happens when a plan is shattered?
evals.expected_output: Plan filename convention: plans/YYYY-MM-DD-{type}-{feature-name}-plan.md. Types: feat, fix, refactor, docs, test, chore. Examples: plans/2026-02-12-feat-user-authentication-plan.md, plans/2026-02-12-fix-checkout-race-condition-plan.md, plans/2026-02-12-refactor-api-client-plan.md. Shattered plans: plans/YYYY-MM-DD-{type}-{feature-name}-shard-N-{phase-name}-plan.md for horizontal shards (independent execution via /rune:strive), or parent plan with children/ subdirectory containing child plans (e.g., plans/children/YYYY-MM-DD-{type}-{name}-child-N-{phase-slug}-plan.md) for hierarchical decomposition (executed via /rune:arc-hierarchy). The feature name is slugified from the user's description.
expectations: States the filename pattern: YYYY-MM-DD-{type}-{feature-name}-plan.md
Lists common types: feat, fix, refactor, docs, test, chore
Provides at least 2 example filenames
Describes shattered plan naming: shard-N suffix
Notes hierarchical uses children/ subdirectory
Mentions feature name is slugified from description
